content.full_text:
City of
FRESNO

TOM BOHIGIAN
Member of the City Council
District 4

TO: Technical Assistance and Analysis Division, Fair
Political Practices Commission
FROM: Councilmember Tom Bohigian
DATE: May 7, 1992
RE: Potential Conflict of Interest

I would deeply appreciate it if the staff could review
this opinion and advise me whether I may participate in
discussions regarding this issue in the future.

City Hall • Fresno, California 93721 • 209 498-1560
District Telephone 209 224-6966

May 6, 1992

MEMORANDUM

TO: Council Member Bohigian

RE: Conflict of Interest

On May 5, 1992 you advised this office that you realized that
same morning that you may have inadvertently participated and
voted on a Council decision on a matter which you may have had a
conflict of interest.

On April 7, 1992, you participated in a discussion and decision
of Council authorizing intervention by the City in litigation
against the Westlands Water District and the expenditure of up to
$21,000 for necessary action relating to said litigation
(hereinafter "Council's decision"). The vote was 6 ayes, 0 noes,
with one Council Member absent. Because you have now recalled
that your wife has received compensation from Westlands Water
District within one year preceding Council's decision, you
advised this office of the foregoing and asked:

     (1) Whether you impermissibly participated in a Council
decision in which you had a conflict of interest.

     (2) If so, whether your participation in the decision
adversely impacts the decision.

     (3) Whether your participation in the decision subjects you
to penalties under the Political Reform Act.

Based on conversations with you and your wife, we understand the
following. Your wife performs free lance writing and public
relations work for both private and public sectors. A few years
ago, she performed services for Westlands Water District. Most
recently she performed work for Westlands Water District again.
She prepared an annual report and other writings and received
compensation exceeding $500 within the one year period prior to
Council's decision. This latter information is reflected in your
most recent statement of Economic Interest.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest:

Under the Political Reform Act (Government Code Section 81000 et
seq. hereinafter "Act"), public officials are disqualified from
participating in governmental decisions in which they know or
have reason to know that they have a financial interest
(Government Code Section 87100) In order to determine whether a

Council Member Bohigian
May 6, 1992
Page 2

financial interest is present, a review of Section 87103 of the
Act is necessary. That section provides in pertinent part:

    An official has a financial interest in a decision within
    the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
    foreseeable that the decision will have a material
    financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
    public generally, on the official or a member of his or her
    immediate family or on:
    . . .
    (c) Any source of income, . . . aggregating two hundred
    fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received
    by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior
    to the time when the decision is made.

Under the Political Reform Act, income includes a public
official's community property interest in a spouse's income as
well as dependent children's income. Thus, the $250 threshold is
met if an official's spouse received $500 of income in the one
year period immediately preceding the decision in issue.

Based on the facts provided in this case, it is unnecessary to
delve into the layers of analysis for determining foreseeability
and material financial effect. The reason for this is that "income" involved is salary received from a local governmental
agency and thereby excepted from Political Reform Act analysis.

In Bach v. McNelis (1989) 207 Cal.App.3d 852, 255 Cal.Rptr. 232,
the Court of Appeal for the Third District was reviewing a case
involving a lawyer's suit against a judge for violating the Act's
income disclosure requirement. The Court sustained a demurrer
based on the following reasoning:

    As we have noted, the term "income" has a special meaning
    under the PRA. As defined there, income "does not
    include:...(2)Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
    diem received from a state, local, or federal government
    agency and reimbursement for travel expenses and per diem
    expenses received from a bona fide educational, academic or
    charitable organizations. (§82030, subd.(b).)

Bach at 866. The compensation received by your wife constitutes
salary from a water district which is a local governmental
agency. For this reason, you did not have a financial interest
under §§87100 and 87103 of the Act.

It might be argued that the exception does not apply since she
received compensation as an independent contractor rather than as
an employee. However, our review of FPPC decisions revealed that

Council Member Bohigian
May 6, 1992
page 3

such a narrow interpretation has not been followed by the FPPC.
For example, the FPPC has rendered an opinion that compensation
received by a director of the Goleta County Water District for
attendance at district meetings "is equivalent to salary" and
"regarded as salary within the meaning of the Act." See In the
Matter of: Opinion Requested by Robert E. Goodwin, Attorney for
Goleta County Water District 1 FPPC Opinions 24. The FPPC
also ruled that pension benefits received by a county retiree
from a county retirement system are "salary" from a local
government agency and, therefore, not "income" under the Act.
See In the Matter of: Opinion Requested by Richard J. Moore,
County Counsel, County of Alameda 3 FPPC Ops. 33.

Impact on Council Decision:

Because we have determined that you did not have an impermissible
conflict of interest under the Act, your participation in the
Council decision did not adversely impact the decision. Assuming
for the sake of argument that you did have such a conflict, we
offer the following analysis. The decision could be subject to
injunctive action pending final adjudication of an action brought
against you or could be deemed void under the following
provisions of the Act:

91003.Injunction.

. . .

(b) Upon a preliminary showing in an action brought by a
person residing in the jurisdiction that a violation ...of
this title or of a disqualification provision of a Conflict
of Interest Code has occurred, the court may restrain the
execution of any official action in relation to which such a
violation occurred, pending final adjudication. If it is
determined that a violation has occurred and that the
official action might not otherwise have been taken or
approved, the court may set the official action aside as
void. The official actions covered by this subsection
include, but are not limited to orders, permits, resolutions
and contracts, but do not include the enactment of any state
legislation. In considering the granting of preliminary or
permanent relief under this subsection, the court shall
accord due weight to any injury that may be suffered by
innocent persons relying on the official action. [emphasis
added]

We do not believe the subject Council decision would be
vulnerable to issuance of the foregoing type of relief by a court
for the following reasons: 1)the action was taken on a six to
zero vote, 2)the decision taken was one adverse to Westlands
Water District, and 3)lack of evidence of injury suffered by

Council Member Bohigian
May 6, 1992
page 4

innocent persons relying on the decision to pursue litigation
(this was a decision to intervene in an action already brought
against the district).

PRA Penalties:

Because we have determined that you did not have a conflict of
interest under the Act, your inquiry as to penalties becomes
moot. Nonetheless, we are attaching a copy of the Act's
enforcement provisions.

CONCLUSION

The salary received by your wife from Westlands Water District
during the one year period immediately preceding April 7, 1992
does not constitute income under the Political Reform Act. For
this reason, you did not have a conflict of interest under the
Act when you participated in Council's decision on April 7, 1992,
relating to intervention in a lawsuit against Westlands Water
District.

FINAL ADMONITION

Please be reminded that you cannot rely on this office's advice
with immunity from enforcement by the FPPC. Only formal, written
advice from the FPPC will provide such protection. Please be
further reminded that you may obtain assistance directly from the
FPPC. The FPPC Technical Assistance number is (916) 322-5662.
In this case, where you have already participated in a decision,
you are still encouraged to seek FPPC assistance.

HARVEY WALLACE
City Attorney

Hilda Cantu Montoy
Assistant

c: Mayor and Council
   Michael A. Bierman
   Harvey Wallace

attachment

sections.conclusion:

For this reason, you did not have a conflict of interest under the Act when you participated in Council’s decision on April 7, 1992, relating to intervention in a lawsuit against Westlands Water District.

FINAL ADMONITION

Please be further reminded that you may obtain assistance directly from the FPPC. The FPPC Technical Assistance number is (916) 322-5662. HARVEY WALLACE  
City Attorney  
[signature] Hilda Cantu Montoy  
Assistant

c: Mayor and Council  
    Michael A. Bierman  
    Harvey Wallace

...

Council Member Bohigian  
May 6, 1992  

innocent persons relying on the decision to pursue litigation (this was a decision to intervene in an action already brought against the district).

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: The salary received by your wife from Westlands Water District during the one year period immediately preceding April 7, 1992 does not constitute income under the Political Reform Act. For this reason, you did not have a conflict of interest under the Act when you participated in Council’s decision on April 7, 1992, relating to intervention in a lawsuit against Westlands Water District.

FINAL ADMONITION

Please be reminded that you cannot rely on this office’s advice with immunity from enforcement by the FPPC. Only formal, written advice from the FPPC will provide such protection. Please be further reminded that you may obtain assistance directly from the FPPC. The FPPC Technical Assistance number is (916) 322-5662. In this case, where you have already participated in a decision, you are still encouraged to seek FPPC assistance.

HARVEY WALLACE  
City Attorney  
[signature] Hilda Cantu Montoy  
Assistant

c: Mayor and Council  
    Michael A. Bierman  
    Harvey Wallace
